I think tomorrow in the leverage section, I reprinted a couple pages from Landrum's book talking about Hefner, that at its peak, at the peak of the power of that brand, they opened all the clubs and Landrum's case is they were right to do that but they shouldn't have run them. They shouldn't have geared up to do that, it's a business they didn't understand, it's a business with pretty thin margins, better they should have licensed. So it's not that it's a bad idea, not that it's the wrong business move but should you or shouldn't you be doing it? And a lot of people just get trapped into the one way, right? And so if we want to be bigger, we got to add outlets. Or if we want to be bigger, we got to add staff, right? And there's many other ways to skin that cat. The discussion about value and I look at what do we do, we create books and tapes, you know, is that real true value creation? But I was thinking intrinsically, just like today, and when thinking about that, you start to think about leveraging place and people because you think about just delivering more intrinsic value and less about the UPV, which I think it was very helpful and instructive. Well, yeah, so first of all, if you take the practical all the way through, what do you really do? You get cars sold that wouldn't get sold otherwise by the dealers that you provide information to. That's what you really do. See, the books and tapes, that's so limited to intrinsic value. So like, see, when I came into that business, you paid hell finding anybody selling anything for more than 70 bucks. I mean, it was the classic Nightingale Conant six tapes and a plastic album with a 16 page workbook, 49.95, you know, and no more than 89.95. Now, earlier incidentally, the normal product was about 3, 400 bucks and it was in a big briefcase, but that all went away. Nightingale made it go away. And there was this ingrained formulaic religion, 10 bucks a tape plus 10 bucks for the workbook. That's how this product is supposed to be priced. And at the time I bought General, because it was the same way, every speaker priced their stuff that way. From Zig to Tommy to you name it, sold from the front of the room, mail order didn't make any difference. And the first time I got Nightingale Conant to sell magnetic marketing for $278, I mean, you would have thought I was asking them to, you know, go cut their mother's eyeballs out and play marbles out in the street and somehow that's going to build their business. I mean, these conversations were, you know, they were just, I mean, it was like a sacrilegious, not just lunatic, but a sacrilegious idea. And of course my position was it had nothing to do with how many tapes are in a thing or how many pages are in, that's not what we're selling here. We're selling an end to the sales guys frustration with cold prospecting and an end to the business owners frustration with getting customers in his store. That's what we're delivering. But if you carry it all the way into this conversation about value today, in the conversation when Rob and Chris were up here, see now you're getting to what I would call the deeper meanings of what we do, right? And so like in your case, see the deeper meaning goes beyond selling more cars for that dealer than would be sold otherwise. The deeper meaning gets to what does that mean to that guy that owns that car dealership? And it means different things to different dealers. Does it mean freedom? Does it mean security? Does it mean pride, ego? Does it, does it mean he's out earning his brother-in-law, the cardiologist to always craps on him every year at Thanksgiving? Does it, what is, what's the deeper meaning of what it is that we do? And that's what really allows you to start to get incredibly price in, fee elastic. I just, I just, a client I worked with for a number of years, some of you heard me mention him, his name was Jack Henry. And Jack taught the grocery store industry and the convenience store industry about employee and delivery man theft and how to control it. And,
